tweets.text:
If you're not aware, Fireblocks is the custody infrastructure that a huge part of institutional crypto runs on.

It already has lots of institutions using it and a ton of volume.

Normally, these integrations don’t mean much to me because they often end up attached to products with barely any users.

But Stacks already has:

→ More than $90M TVL
→ BTC-native trading and liquidity through Bitflow
→ Active ecosystem (Zest, Hermetica, Granite)

Institutions now have a way to get yield on their idle BTC. No more excuses about tooling or custody.

Disclosure: I'm holding $STX.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Stacks is now live on @FireblocksHQ.

2,400+ institutional clients can now reach Bitcoin DeFi directly: lending, trading, and BTC yield using the custody infrastructure they already trust. https://t.co/VOoY3qQk5y

tweets.text: there’s nothing magical about OTC trades. the desk still needs to source demand/supply from somewhere. when making trades of big size they’re going to make a splash regardless of how you carry them out. i’m not aware of anyone who’s in the market for 50,000 coins at $63,000 right now.

thread_tweets.text: there’s nothing magical about OTC trades. the desk still needs to source demand/supply from somewhere. when making trades of big size they’re going to make a splash regardless of how you carry them out. i’m not aware of anyone who’s in the market for 50,000 coins at $63,000 right now.
thread_tweets.full_text: there’s nothing magical about OTC trades. the desk still needs to source demand/supply from somewhere. when making trades of big size they’re going to make a splash regardless of how you carry them out. i’m not aware of anyone who’s in the market for 50,000 coins at $63,000 right now.

thread_tweets.text:
it’s a question of liquidity

in september last year for example the market was way more liquid, there was way more money changing hands at the time, it was more conceivable you could match buyers and sellers at a specific price point even in large quantities. order books can do that too btw it’s just a question of market depth, on the books or off the books

also some of the big OTC trades last year were at least partially executed by the desks on open order books. the OTC desk just sources liquidity from wherever they think they can get the best price. sometimes that’s an order book

tldr the reason you could buy a lot at the top without moving the price is because there were a lot of sellers. the reason you can’t sell a lot right now without moving the price is because there aren’t a lot of buyers

tweets.text:
If you’ve noticed, I rarely publish new equity ideas these days.

The reason is simple: I only invest when I believe I have a genuine edge, not when I’m competing with 20 analysts covering the same company and all of us are looking at the same information.

Most ideas are noise. A few are opportunities.

I believe one of those opportunities may be approaching.

Over the next few weeks, we could get what I believe would be one of the best Buy and Hold opportunities of the year in a high-quality company with a market cap between $2 billion and $5 billion.

What makes this setup unique is that I have access to what I believe is the best investor covering this company, along with industry sources whose understanding of the situation is far superior to anything available in public research.

It’s similar to the edge that led me to recommend $WGS, which is up roughly 50% since then. In this case, I believe the information advantage may be even greater.

However, one thing needs to happen first.

If it does, I expect to allocate roughly 23% of my portfolio to the opportunity, I already put aside cash. I’ll publish it immediately after and explain exactly why I bought it and what I believe the market is missing.

Stay tuned.
tweets.full_text:
If you’ve noticed, I rarely publish new equity ideas these days.

The reason is simple: I only invest when I believe I have a genuine edge, not when I’m competing with 20 analysts covering the same company and all of us are looking at the same information.

Most ideas are noise. A few are opportunities.

I believe one of those opportunities may be approaching.

Over the next few weeks, we could get what I believe would be one of the best Buy and Hold opportunities of the year in a high-quality company with a market cap between $2 billion and $5 billion.

What makes this setup unique is that I have access to what I believe is the best investor covering this company, along with industry sources whose understanding of the situation is far superior to anything available in public research.

It’s similar to the edge that led me to recommend $WGS, which is up roughly 50% since then. In this case, I believe the information advantage may be even greater.

However, one thing needs to happen first.

If it does, I expect to allocate roughly 23% of my portfolio to the opportunity, I already put aside cash. I’ll publish it immediately after and explain exactly why I bought it and what I believe the market is missing.

Stay tuned.

tweets.text:
HyperScaler Prisoner’s Dilemma

The AI spending race is starting to look like a classic Prisoner’s Dilemma.

$MSFT, $META, $AMZN, and $GOOGL would probably all benefit if they collectively slowed capex growth and focused on profits. But nobody wants to be the first to blink and risk falling behind.

As long as the arms race continues, $MU and $SNDK are the obvious beneficiaries.

The risk? Wall Street keeps upgrading memory stocks as if AI spending can only go up. If the hyperscalers ever decide that enough is enough, the same consensus that pushed estimates higher could get crushed just as quickly.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Goldman: "the first hyperscaler to signal that it can slow the pace of spending will likely see its share price rewarded. If that happens, others will take notice. That is the reflexivity that ultimately stalls the capex cycle" https://t.co/yXcil2Ztjt
tweets.full_text:
HyperScaler Prisoner’s Dilemma

The AI spending race is starting to look like a classic Prisoner’s Dilemma.

$MSFT, $META, $AMZN, and $GOOGL would probably all benefit if they collectively slowed capex growth and focused on profits. But nobody wants to be the first to blink and risk falling behind.

As long as the arms race continues, $MU and $SNDK are the obvious beneficiaries.

The risk? Wall Street keeps upgrading memory stocks as if AI spending can only go up. If the hyperscalers ever decide that enough is enough, the same consensus that pushed estimates higher could get crushed just as quickly.

tweets.text:
I am so confused.

Just a few months ago, every expert and almost every account on X was telling us the same thing: the dollar was dead. De-dollarization was inevitable. The reserve currency was finished. The end was near.

Scroll back and try to find a dollar bull. Good luck.

It makes you wonder: how valuable is all the information we consume every day? How much weight should we really give to experts, forecasts, and endless threads that sound convincing at the time?

The older I get, the more I realize that the consensus is often just a reflection of what already happened, not what is about to happen.

Maybe the lesson isn’t to find better forecasters.

Maybe the lesson is to stop listening to forecasts altogether.

thread_tweets.text:
Governments can approach the companies building AI and ask what would make it worth their while.

The state doesn't have to pay for this. We can make deals that are immensely positive sum.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Governments can approach the companies building AI and ask what would make it worth their while.

The state doesn't have to pay for this. We can make deals that are immensely positive sum.

thread_tweets.text: Government and companies can work together to steer AI so that it is maximally beneficial to society, and to move quickly to put helpful AI in the hands of every American, which will no doubt begin to change how many of them feel about the industry as a whole.
thread_tweets.full_text: Government and companies can work together to steer AI so that it is maximally beneficial to society, and to move quickly to put helpful AI in the hands of every American, which will no doubt begin to change how many of them feel about the industry as a whole.

tweets.full_text:
In short order, we can work to build at a minimum three AI assistants for every citizen. One medical. One legal. One educational.

Genuinely competent, and essentially free.

Ultra-wealthy people have access to top doctors, effective lawyers, and best-in-class tutors for their kids. 

What if we could make that available for every American? We're well within reach of that future, and government + companies can collaborate to get there.

Governments can ask for these agents, and access for Americans in return.

What the companies want is real frameworks and real collaboration. 

Clear permitting on a data center (once jobs, power, and the local community are squared away). A safe harbor to innovate in healthcare, set by America's hospital systems.

Governments can approach the companies building AI and ask what would make it worth their while.

The state doesn't have to pay for this. We can make deals that are immensely positive sum.

Government and companies can work together to steer AI so that it is maximally beneficial to society, and to move quickly to put helpful AI in the hands of every American, which will no doubt begin to change how many of them feel about the industry as a whole.

tweets.text:
There are some interesting rules of thumbs when hiring someone:

“Good executives free up your time. Bad hires consume all of it.”

“Every person you hire should reduce your workload by at least 5%.”

“The purpose of hiring is to buy back your time.”

Although I have misremembered it, I generally follow the mental model of: 

“Each hire should take up less than 5% of your time.” 

The variant allows managers to hire people to work on new initiatives, not just but back their time. Importantly, the hire should be autonomous enough not to consume a significantly amount of your time. 

It might be a very phd / professor mindset; who typically do everything in their power to protect their own time, so they can focus on fun work like research.
